---
title: "bulk_extractor Analysis Tutorial"
subtitle: "CSCI 4623 — Digital Forensics"
format:
  closeread-html:
    css: be-tutorial.css
    cr-style:
      narrative-sidebar-width: "2fr"
---

```{=html}
<div class="tutorial-meta">
<strong>Estimated Time:</strong> 60–75 minutes &nbsp;|&nbsp;
<strong>Prerequisites:</strong> Linux with <code>bulk_extractor</code>, The Sleuth Kit, and <code>exiftool</code> installed &nbsp;|&nbsp;
<strong>Setup Script:</strong> <a href="setup_be_lab.sh" download>setup_be_lab.sh</a>
</div>
```

## Introduction

This tutorial walks you through stream-based forensic extraction using **bulk_extractor**.
Where TSK navigates filesystem structures to find files, bulk_extractor treats a disk image
as a flat stream of bytes, scanning every page for recognizable patterns — email addresses,
credit card numbers, URLs, GPS coordinates, and dozens of other artifact types. It finds
things hidden in compressed archives, deleted space, swap regions, and slack — places that
filesystem-aware tools never look.

By the end of this tutorial you will be able to run bulk_extractor against a disk image,
interpret its output (feature files, histograms, carved data), control which scanners run,
create stop lists, and bridge bulk_extractor results back to filesystem context using
`identify_filenames.py`.

### Setup

Run the setup script as root to create the practice image:

```bash
sudo bash setup_be_lab.sh
```

This produces `be_lab.dd` — a 128 MiB raw image with a single ext3 partition containing
planted forensic artifacts: emails, phone numbers, credit cards, URLs, a JPEG with GPS
metadata, a gzip archive with hidden PII, a BASE64-encoded memo, and a file that was
deleted after creation. Record the deleted file's inode number from the script output —
you will need it in Task 11.

Verify the image exists:

```bash
ls -lh be_lab.dd
```

You should see a 128M file. If bulk_extractor is not installed, install it:

```bash
sudo apt install bulk-extractor    # Kali / Ubuntu
```


<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- TASK 0 — Orientation                                        -->
<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

```{=html}
<div class="sticky-header"><h2>Task 0: Orientation — Know Your Image</h2></div>
```

Before running bulk_extractor, get a baseline understanding of what the filesystem
contains. This gives you ground truth to compare against bulk_extractor's output later.

::::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-fsstat}
```
$ fsstat -o 2048 be_lab.dd

FILE SYSTEM INFORMATION
--------------------------------------------
File System Type: Ext3
Volume Name: suspect_drive
Volume ID: [...]

CONTENT INFORMATION
--------------------------------------------
Block Range: 0 - 32255
Block Size: 1024
[...]
```
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-fsstat"}
Run `fsstat` with the partition offset (2048 sectors) to confirm the filesystem type and
layout. The volume label `suspect_drive` confirms you have the right image. Note the
block size — 1024 bytes — which differs from bulk_extractor's 16 MiB page size.
:::

:::{#cr-fls}
```
$ fls -o 2048 -r be_lab.dd

d/d 11: lost+found
d/d 12: documents
r/r 13: documents/contacts.txt
r/r 14: documents/financial_records.txt
r/r 15: documents/archived_log.gz
r/r 16: documents/encoded_memo.b64
r/- 17: documents/destroy_after_reading.txt  (deleted)
d/d 18: web_cache
r/r 19: web_cache/browser_history.txt
d/d 20: personal
r/r 21: personal/diary.txt
r/r 22: personal/meetup_photo.jpg
r/r 23: personal/.swap_fragment
```
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-fls"}
Now list the files with `fls -r`. Notice the allocated files and the one deleted entry
(inode 17, marked with `(deleted)`). This is the file whose content persists in
unallocated space. bulk_extractor will find its artifacts even though the filesystem
no longer points to it.

Count the files: you can see the `.gz` archive, the `.b64` file, the JPEG, and the
swap fragment. Keep this listing handy — you will use it to verify that bulk_extractor
finds artifacts from every planted source, including the deleted one.
:::

::::

::: {.try-it}
**Try it:** How many regular files does `fls` show (including the deleted one)? What
artifact types would you expect to find in `contacts.txt` vs. `financial_records.txt`?
:::


<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- TASK 1 — First Full Scan                                    -->
<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

```{=html}
<div class="sticky-header"><h2>Task 1: First Full Scan</h2></div>
```

::::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-firstscan}
```
$ bulk_extractor -o be_output be_lab.dd

bulk_extractor version: 2.1.1
Input file: be_lab.dd
Output directory: be_output
Disk Size: 134217728
Threads: 4
Phase 1.

13:�:15 Offset 0MB (0.00%) Done in n/a
13:45:16 Offset 128MB (100.00%) Done in  0:00:01
All data are read; waiting for�workers to finish...

Phase 2. Shutting down scanners
Phase 3. Creating Histograms
   email_histogram.txt
   domain_histogram.txt
   url_histogram.txt
   [...]

Elapsed time: 0:00:02
Overall performance: 64.000 MBytes/sec
```
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-firstscan"}
This is the fundamental invocation: `bulk_extractor -o <output_dir> <image>`. The `-o`
flag specifies where all output goes — bulk_extractor will refuse to run if this
directory already exists (a safety measure against overwriting evidence).

Watch the three processing phases in the output. **Phase 1** reads the image in
overlapping 16 MiB pages and dispatches them to scanner threads. **Phase 2** shuts
down scanners and flushes buffered results. **Phase 3** sorts feature files and
builds frequency histograms — the "executive summary" of what was found.

On this small 128 MiB image, the scan completes in seconds. On a 500 GB drive with
all scanners enabled, expect 30–90 minutes depending on hardware and thread count.
:::

::::

::: {.try-it}
**Try it:** What happens if you try to run the same command again without deleting
`be_output/` first? Try it and note the error message — this is an intentional
forensic safeguard.
:::


<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- TASK 2 — Explore Output Directory                           -->
<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

```{=html}
<div class="sticky-header"><h2>Task 2: Explore the Output Directory</h2></div>
```

::::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-outputdir}
```
$ ls -la be_output/

total 248
drwxr-xr-x  4 root root  4096 ... .
drwxr-xr-x  3 root root  4096 ... ..
-rw-r--r--  1 root root   412 ... alerts.txt
-rw-r--r--  1 root root  1893 ... ccn.txt
-rw-r--r--  1 root root     0 ... ccn_track2.txt
-rw-r--r--  1 root root  2341 ... domain.txt
-rw-r--r--  1 root root   891 ... domain_histogram.txt
-rw-r--r--  1 root root  4102 ... email.txt
-rw-r--r--  1 root root   723 ... email_histogram.txt
-rw-r--r--  1 root root  1204 ... exif.txt
-rw-r--r--  1 root root     0 ... find.txt
drwxr-xr-x  2 root root  4096 ... jpeg/
-rw-r--r--  1 root root  8924 ... report.xml
-rw-r--r--  1 root root     0 ... stopped.txt
-rw-r--r--  1 root root  1567 ... telephone.txt
-rw-r--r--  1 root root  3218 ... url.txt
-rw-r--r--  1 root root   934 ... url_histogram.txt
drwxr-xr-x  2 root root  4096 ... unzip/
-rw-r--r--  1 root root     0 ... windirs.txt
[...]
```
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-outputdir"}
Every scan produces this standard structure. The key file types are **feature files**
(`.txt` — one per artifact category), **histograms** (`*_histogram.txt` — frequency
counts), **carved data directories** (`jpeg/`, `unzip/`), and the **provenance record**
(`report.xml` in DFXML format).

Notice `stopped.txt` — if any scanner crashes or is manually stopped, it gets logged
here. An empty file means everything ran cleanly. Similarly, `alerts.txt` logs anything
the scanners flagged as especially noteworthy.
:::

:::{#cr-reportxml}
```
$ head -30 be_output/report.xml

<?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8'?>
<dfxml xmloutputversion='1.0'>
  <creator version='1.0'>
    <program>BULK_EXTRACTOR</program>
    <version>2.1.1</version>
  </creator>
  <source>
    <image_filename>be_lab.dd</image_filename>
    <image_size>134217728</image_size>
  </source>
  <configuration>
    <scanners>
      <scanner name="accts">ENABLED</scanner>
      <scanner name="base64">ENABLED</scanner>
      <scanner name="email">ENABLED</scanner>
      <scanner name="exif">ENABLED</scanner>
      <scanner name="gzip">ENABLED</scanner>
      [...]
    </scanners>
  </configuration>
  [...]
</dfxml>
```
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-reportxml"}
The `report.xml` file is your chain-of-custody record for the scan itself. It documents
exactly which version of bulk_extractor ran, what image was scanned, which scanners
were enabled, and the start/end timestamps. In a real investigation, this file goes
into your case documentation alongside the image hash.

Notice how each scanner is listed with its status. This tells you (and any reviewer)
exactly what analysis was performed.
:::

::::


<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- TASK 3 — Read Feature Files                                 -->
<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

```{=html}
<div class="sticky-header"><h2>Task 3: Read Feature Files</h2></div>
```

::::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-emailtxt}
```
$ cat be_output/email.txt

# Feature-File-Version: 1.1
# Feature-Recorder: email
49152	jsmith@forensiclab.org	Team Contact List — Project Nightfall\x0a=====
49231	maria.garcia@evidence.net	Field tech:      maria.garcia@evidence.net
49298	bob.chen@lawfirm.com	Legal counsel:   bob.chen@lawfirm.com
49358	darkweb_dealer99@protonmail.ch	External:        darkweb_dealer99@proton
49487	suspect_alias@tutanota.com	via suspect_alias@tutanota.com\x0aand was
49552	throwaway_42@guerrillamail.com	using throwaway_42@guerrillamail.com.
1048576-GZIP-0	operator_x@securemail.onion	--- ENCRYPTED COMMUNICATION LOG
1048576-GZIP-87	handler99@riseup.net	To:   handler99@riseup.net\x0aDate:
1048576-GZIP-192	burner_phone@signal.me	Backup contact: burner_phone@signal.me
1098752-BASE64-52	insider_trade@corpmail.com	flagged for review:\x0a  -
1098752-BASE64-101	leak_source@whistleblow.org	external communication
1098752-BASE64-143	cfo_backup@gmail.com	unauthorized cloud storage
2162688	ghost_op@tutanota.com	Contact: ghost_op@tutanota.com  Backup:
8421376	tracking_updates@gmail.com	tracking to myself: tracking_updates@gmail
8454144	k_drops@darkmail.de	contact him at k_drops@darkmail.de\x0aif
16384000	admin_access@rootkit.cc	SWAP FRAGMENT: admin_access@rootkit.cc
```
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-emailtxt"}
This is the core output format: a **three-column TSV** (tab-separated values). Every
feature file follows this structure:

  - **Column 1 — Forensic path:** the byte offset (or nested decompression path)
    where the artifact was found.
  - **Column 2 — Feature:** the extracted artifact itself.
  - **Column 3 — Context:** surrounding bytes to help you understand where the
    artifact appeared.

Look at the first entries — offsets like `49152` and `49231` are simple byte positions
in the raw image. These come from `contacts.txt`, which sits in an allocated filesystem
block.
:::

Now look at the entries with complex forensic paths. The path
<span class="forensic-path">1048576-GZIP-0</span> means: "go to byte 1,048,576 in
the image, decompress the GZIP stream found there, and the artifact is at offset 0
within the decompressed data." This is the content of `archived_log.gz` — bulk_extractor
automatically decompressed it and scanned the contents. @cr-emailtxt

Similarly, <span class="forensic-path">1098752-BASE64-52</span> means bulk_extractor
detected BASE64 encoding at byte 1,098,752, decoded it, and found an email address
52 bytes into the decoded stream. This is the `encoded_memo.b64` file — again, no
manual decoding needed. @cr-emailtxt

The entry at offset `2162688` is especially interesting — look at its context:
`ghost_op@tutanota.com`. Cross-reference with `fls` output: this offset falls
*beyond the allocated files*. This is the **deleted** `destroy_after_reading.txt`.
bulk_extractor found it because it scans *every byte*, not just allocated space.
@cr-emailtxt

The entry at `16384000` (`admin_access@rootkit.cc`) came from the planted swap
fragment — data hidden among random bytes that no filesystem tool would surface.
@cr-emailtxt

::::

::: {.concept-box}
**Key concept:** Feature files are bulk_extractor's primary output. The three-column
format — forensic path, feature, context — is consistent across all feature files.
Learning to read this format fluently is the single most important skill in this tutorial.
:::

::: {.try-it}
**Try it:** Examine `telephone.txt` and `url.txt` the same way. Can you match each
phone number back to the file it came from? Which URLs came from `browser_history.txt`
vs. other sources?
:::


<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- TASK 4 — Forensic Paths in Depth                            -->
<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

```{=html}
<div class="sticky-header"><h2>Task 4: Interpret Forensic Paths</h2></div>
```

::::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-fpaths}
| Forensic Path | Meaning |
|:---|:---|
| `49152` | Simple: byte offset 49152 in the raw image |
| `1048576-GZIP-0` | At image byte 1048576, decompress GZIP → artifact at offset 0 |
| `1048576-GZIP-87` | Same GZIP stream, artifact at decoded offset 87 |
| `1098752-BASE64-52` | At image byte 1098752, decode BASE64 → artifact at offset 52 |
| `500000-GZIP-0-BASE64-128` | Nested: GZIP at 500000 → decode BASE64 → offset 128 |
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-fpaths"}
Forensic paths are bulk_extractor's coordinate system. A simple numeric offset tells
you exactly where on disk an artifact lives — you can jump to it with
`dd if=be_lab.dd bs=1 skip=49152 count=100` to see the raw bytes.

Compound paths record each decompression or decoding layer. Reading left to right:
start at the image offset, apply each transformation in sequence, then the final
number is the offset within the innermost decoded stream.

In real-world cases, paths can nest deeply: a ZIP file inside a hibernation file
inside a disk image might produce something like
<span class="forensic-path">83886080-HIBER-0-ZIP-0-GZIP-1024</span>. Each segment
is a breadcrumb leading you back to the original bytes.
:::

Knowing the forensic path lets you verify findings independently. For simple offsets,
extract the raw bytes:

```bash
dd if=be_lab.dd bs=1 skip=49152 count=200 2>/dev/null | strings
```

For compressed paths, you need the original container. The image offset
(first number) tells you where the compressed blob lives — you can extract it with
`dd`, decompress manually, and verify the artifact at the given sub-offset. @cr-fpaths

::::

::: {.try-it}
**Try it:** Pick an email from `email.txt` that has a simple numeric offset. Use `dd`
and `strings` to verify you can see it at that location in the raw image. Then try
one with a `GZIP` path — can you find the `.gz` file at that offset?
:::


<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- TASK 5 — Histogram Analysis                                 -->
<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

```{=html}
<div class="sticky-header"><h2>Task 5: Histogram Analysis</h2></div>
```

::::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-emailhist}
```
$ cat be_output/email_histogram.txt

# Histogram-File-Version: 1.1
# Feature-Recorder: email
n=2	jsmith@forensiclab.org
n=1	maria.garcia@evidence.net
n=1	bob.chen@lawfirm.com
n=1	darkweb_dealer99@protonmail.ch
n=1	suspect_alias@tutanota.com
n=1	throwaway_42@guerrillamail.com
n=1	operator_x@securemail.onion
n=1	handler99@riseup.net
n=1	burner_phone@signal.me
n=1	insider_trade@corpmail.com
n=1	leak_source@whistleblow.org
n=1	cfo_backup@gmail.com
n=1	ghost_op@tutanota.com
n=1	tracking_updates@gmail.com
n=1	k_drops@darkmail.de
n=1	admin_access@rootkit.cc
```
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-emailhist"}
Histograms rank artifacts by frequency. The format is `n=<count>` followed by the
feature value. On a real 500 GB drive, the email histogram might contain thousands
of entries — the investigative signal is in the *distribution*.

**High-frequency entries** are usually noise: system accounts, automated notifications,
web framework addresses baked into cached pages. On a production image, seeing
`noreply@facebook.com` with `n=847` tells you nothing useful.

**Low-frequency entries** are often interesting: a unique email appearing once or
twice may be a personal contact, a suspect communication, or a one-time-use address.
In our lab image every address appears only once or twice, but on real media you
would filter aggressively.

Think of histograms as the **executive summary**. An investigator reviewing a new
case might spend ten minutes reading histograms before ever opening a feature file.
The frequency distribution tells you what is ordinary and what stands out.
:::

:::{#cr-domainhist}
```
$ cat be_output/domain_histogram.txt

n=3	gmail.com
n=2	tutanota.com
n=1	forensiclab.org
n=1	evidence.net
n=1	lawfirm.com
n=1	protonmail.ch
n=1	guerrillamail.com
n=1	securemail.onion
n=1	riseup.net
n=1	signal.me
n=1	corpmail.com
n=1	whistleblow.org
n=1	darkmail.de
n=1	rootkit.cc
```
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-domainhist"}
The domain histogram aggregates email domains. Notice what jumps out: `securemail.onion`
(a Tor hidden service), `riseup.net` and `tutanota.com` (privacy-focused providers),
`guerrillamail.com` (disposable email), `rootkit.cc` (suspicious TLD and name).

On a real case, this histogram immediately reveals the suspect's communication
preferences. Privacy-focused services in combination with disposable addresses
paint a pattern of operational security awareness.
:::

::::

::: {.try-it}
**Try it:** Examine `url_histogram.txt`. Which domains appear? Do any URLs point to
internal/private IP addresses (like 192.168.x.x)? What might that tell an
investigator?
:::


<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- TASK 6 — Targeted Scanning                                  -->
<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

```{=html}
<div class="sticky-header"><h2>Task 6: Targeted Scan — accts Scanner Only</h2></div>
```

::::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-targeted}
```
$ bulk_extractor -o be_accts_only -E accts be_lab.dd

bulk_extractor version: 2.1.1
[...]
Phase 1.
13:52:01 Offset 128MB (100.00%) Done in  0:00:00
Phase 2. Shutting down scanners
Phase 3. Creating Histograms
   ccn_histogram.txt

Elapsed time: 0:00:01
Overall performance: 128.000 MBytes/sec
```
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-targeted"}
The `-E` flag means *exclusive* — run **only** the named scanner(s), disabling
everything else. Compare the elapsed time and output: far fewer files are created
because only the `accts` scanner ran.

This is useful when you know exactly what you are looking for. If the case involves
financial fraud, running only `accts` (credit cards, bank routing numbers) gives
you results in a fraction of the time. On a 500 GB image, this can mean minutes
instead of an hour.

The related flags are:

  - `-e scanner` — enable a scanner that is off by default
  - `-x scanner` — disable a specific scanner
  - `-E scanner` — exclusive mode: *only* this scanner runs
  - `-H` — list all available scanners and their default status
:::

```bash
# List all scanners to see what's available:
bulk_extractor -H
```

Run this now. You will see scanners grouped by function: pattern-based (email, accts),
structure-aware (exif, elf), network (net), decompression (gzip, zip, base64), and
utility (wordlist, find). @cr-targeted

::::

::: {.try-it}
**Try it:** Run a scan with only the `email` scanner enabled (`-E email`). Compare
the output directory contents against the full scan — which files are missing?
How much faster was it?
:::


<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- TASK 7 — Custom Pattern Search                              -->
<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

```{=html}
<div class="sticky-header"><h2>Task 7: Custom Pattern Search with -F</h2></div>
```

::::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-customfind}
```
$ cat suspect_patterns.txt
ghost_op
nightfall
east dock
rootkit

$ bulk_extractor -o be_find -F suspect_patterns.txt be_lab.dd
[...]

$ cat be_find/find.txt

# Feature-File-Version: 1.1
# Feature-Recorder: find
2162700	ghost_op	Contact: ghost_op@tutanota.com  Backup:
2162780	nightfall	reference: NIGHTFALL-FINAL\x0a\x0aDestroy
8421388	east dock	Meeting location — east dock warehouse
16384032	rootkit	SWAP FRAGMENT: admin_access@rootkit.cc
```
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-customfind"}
The **find scanner** searches for literal strings or regular expressions you provide.
Create a text file with one pattern per line, then pass it with `-F`.

This is invaluable for case-specific searches. If you know a suspect uses certain
aliases, codenames, or phrases, you can search the entire disk — including compressed
and encoded data — for those exact strings.

For inline one-off searches, use `-f pattern` instead of `-F file`:

```bash
bulk_extractor -o be_find2 -f "nightfall" be_lab.dd
```

The results appear in `find.txt` using the same three-column feature file format.
Notice how `ghost_op` was found at offset 2162700 — this is in the unallocated space
where the deleted file's content persists. The pattern search found a suspect alias
in data the filesystem no longer references.
:::

::::

::: {.concept-box}
**Key concept:** The `-f` / `-F` flags turn bulk_extractor into a high-performance
grep that works across compressed data, encoded content, and unallocated space. On
a large image, this is dramatically faster than `strings | grep` because it leverages
bulk_extractor's parallel page model.
:::


<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- TASK 8 — Carved Data                                        -->
<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

```{=html}
<div class="sticky-header"><h2>Task 8: Carved Data — JPEG Analysis</h2></div>
```

::::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-carved}
```
$ ls be_output/jpeg/

00008192.jpg

$ exiftool be_output/jpeg/00008192.jpg

ExifTool Version Number         : 12.76
File Name                       : 00008192.jpg
[...]
Make                            : Samsung
Camera Model Name               : Galaxy S23 Ultra
Date/Time Original              : 2025:09:14 22:30:15
GPS Latitude                    : 33 deg 44' 56.40" N
GPS Longitude                   : 84 deg 23' 16.80" W
Artist                          : J. Smith
Image Description               : Meeting location — east dock warehouse
[...]
```
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-carved"}
bulk_extractor's EXIF scanner carved this JPEG from the image and placed it in the
`jpeg/` subdirectory. The filename (`00008192.jpg`) reflects the byte offset where
the JPEG header was found.

Run `exiftool` on the carved image. The EXIF metadata reveals critical investigative
details: the photo was taken with a Samsung Galaxy S23 Ultra on September 14, 2025
at 10:30 PM. The GPS coordinates (33.749° N, 84.388° W) place the camera at a
specific physical location. The image description and artist field add context.

This kind of metadata extraction happens automatically on every JPEG bulk_extractor
encounters — whether the file is allocated, deleted, in slack space, or embedded
inside a compressed archive.

The EXIF data also appears in `exif.txt`:

```bash
cat be_output/exif.txt
```

Here you will see the same information in feature-file format, with forensic paths
pointing to the exact byte offsets of each EXIF tag in the image.
:::

::::

::: {.try-it}
**Try it:** The GPS coordinates are in the carved EXIF data. Convert them to decimal
degrees and look them up on a map. What location do they indicate? How would this
information be useful in a real investigation?
:::


<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- TASK 9 — Stop Lists                                         -->
<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

```{=html}
<div class="sticky-header"><h2>Task 9: Stop Lists — Filtering Known-Good Artifacts</h2></div>
```

::::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-stoplist}
```
# Concept: scan a clean baseline, use its output as a stop list

# 1. Create a minimal "clean" image for comparison
$ dd if=/dev/zero of=clean.dd bs=1M count=32 status=none
$ printf 'o\nn\np\n1\n2048\n\nt\n83\nw\n' | fdisk clean.dd >/dev/null 2>&1
$ LOOP=$(losetup --find --show --offset $((2048*512)) clean.dd)
$ mkfs.ext3 -q $LOOP
$ mkdir -p /mnt/clean && mount $LOOP /mnt/clean

# Put known-good content (OS files, default config, etc.)
$ echo "Default system readme" > /mnt/clean/README
$ umount /mnt/clean && losetup -d $LOOP

# 2. Scan the clean image
$ bulk_extractor -o clean_output clean.dd

# 3. Re-scan suspect image using clean output as stop list
$ bulk_extractor -o be_filtered -w clean_output be_lab.dd
```
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-stoplist"}
A **stop list** is a set of known-good artifacts to exclude from results. The workflow
mirrors block hash filtering: scan a known-clean baseline, then subtract those artifacts
from the suspect image's results.

The `-w` flag points to a previous bulk_extractor output directory. During Phase 3,
bulk_extractor checks each feature against the stop list's histogram files. Any feature
found in the stop list is omitted from the final output.

In practice, you would build stop lists from a clean install of the same OS version
the suspect used. This removes thousands of noise entries — email addresses embedded
in system libraries, URLs in browser defaults, phone number patterns in locale files.
What remains is content unique to the suspect's activity.

For our lab image, the clean baseline is trivially small and won't filter much. On
a real case, a properly built stop list can reduce feature file sizes by 80–90%.
:::

::::

::: {.concept-box}
**Key concept:** Stop lists are the bulk_extractor equivalent of NSRL/RDS hash filtering.
Both implement the same forensic principle: subtract the known to surface the unknown.
The difference is granularity — hash filtering works at the file level; stop lists work
at the artifact level.
:::


<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- TASK 10 — identify_filenames.py                             -->
<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

```{=html}
<div class="sticky-header"><h2>Task 10: Bridging to Filesystem Context</h2></div>
```

::::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-identify}
```
# 1. Generate DFXML with fiwalk
$ fiwalk -X be_lab_dfxml.xml be_lab.dd

# 2. Annotate feature files with filenames
$ python3 identify_filenames.py --all \
    --xmlfile be_lab_dfxml.xml \
    be_output/

# 3. Check the annotated output
$ cat be_output/annotated/email.txt

49152	jsmith@forensiclab.org	[...] documents/contacts.txt
49231	maria.garcia@evidence.net	[...] documents/contacts.txt
[...]
2162688	ghost_op@tutanota.com	[...] (unallocated)
[...]
```
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-identify"}
This is the critical integration point between bulk_extractor and filesystem-aware tools.
The problem: bulk_extractor gives you byte offsets, but investigators need to know
*which file* an artifact came from.

**`fiwalk`** (part of TSK) walks the filesystem and produces a DFXML document mapping
every file to its byte ranges on disk. **`identify_filenames.py`** (bundled with
bulk_extractor) reads this mapping and annotates each feature with the corresponding
filename.

After annotation, the email at offset 49152 is tagged with `documents/contacts.txt` —
now you know exactly which file it came from. Features from unallocated space get
marked `(unallocated)`, telling you the source file was deleted.

This bridges the gap between bulk_extractor's stream-based view and TSK's
filesystem-aware view. Neither tool alone gives the full picture:

  - bulk_extractor finds artifacts TSK cannot see (compressed, encoded, unallocated)
  - TSK provides file attribution bulk_extractor cannot provide
  - Together, every artifact gets both a location and a context
:::

::::

::: {.warning-box}
**Note:** `identify_filenames.py` ships with bulk_extractor but may not be in your
`$PATH`. Check `/usr/share/bulk_extractor/` or the source repository's `python/`
directory. If `fiwalk` is not installed, `apt install sleuthkit` provides it.
:::

::: {.try-it}
**Try it:** After running `identify_filenames.py`, examine the annotated email file.
Which emails map to allocated files? Which are marked as unallocated? Does this match
what you expected from the `fls` listing in Task 0?
:::


<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- TASK 11 — Deleted File Recovery                             -->
<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

```{=html}
<div class="sticky-header"><h2>Task 11: Deleted File Recovery via Byte Offsets</h2></div>
```

::::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-deleted}
```
# From email.txt, we saw ghost_op@tutanota.com at offset 2162688
# This offset falls outside any allocated file

# Verify with dd:
$ dd if=be_lab.dd bs=1 skip=2162688 count=256 2>/dev/null | strings

EYES ONLY — DELETE IMMEDIATELY

Meeting confirmed for 2025-09-15 at 0200 hours.
Contact: ghost_op@tutanota.com  Backup: 555-000-1234

Wire $50,000 to account 9876543210, routing 071000013.
Use reference: NIGHTFALL-FINAL

Destroy this message.

# Cross-reference with TSK: use the inode number from setup script
$ icat -o 2048 be_lab.dd <DELETED_INODE>

EYES ONLY — DELETE IMMEDIATELY
[... same content ...]
```
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-deleted"}
This task demonstrates the investigative power of combining bulk_extractor with TSK.
bulk_extractor found the email `ghost_op@tutanota.com` at byte offset 2162688.
The `identify_filenames.py` annotation (or manual cross-referencing) reveals this
offset is in unallocated space — the file was deleted.

Using the raw offset, `dd` extracts the bytes directly. You can see the full content
of the deleted file: meeting times, phone numbers, wire transfer details, and the
codename "NIGHTFALL-FINAL."

If you recorded the deleted inode from the setup script, `icat` recovers the same
content through the filesystem metadata path. Both methods reach the same data — one
via raw byte offset (bulk_extractor's worldview), the other via inode number
(TSK's worldview).

This is the pattern that makes bulk_extractor invaluable in real investigations. The
suspect deleted a file believing it was gone. bulk_extractor's stream scan found
every artifact in it regardless.
:::

::::


<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- TASK 12 — Trace GZIP Decompression Path                     -->
<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

```{=html}
<div class="sticky-header"><h2>Task 12: Trace a Forensic Path Through GZIP</h2></div>
```

::::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-gziptrace}
```
# email.txt shows: 1048576-GZIP-0  operator_x@securemail.onion
# Let's trace this path manually.

# 1. Extract bytes starting at image offset 1048576
$ dd if=be_lab.dd bs=1 skip=1048576 count=4096 2>/dev/null | \
    file -

/dev/stdin: gzip compressed data [...]

# 2. Extract and decompress
$ dd if=be_lab.dd bs=1 skip=1048576 count=4096 2>/dev/null | \
    gunzip 2>/dev/null | head -5

--- ENCRYPTED COMMUNICATION LOG (recovered) ---

From: operator_x@securemail.onion
To:   handler99@riseup.net
Date: 2025-09-10

# 3. The "-0" means offset 0 in the decompressed stream
#    — the email starts at the very beginning of the decoded data.
```
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-gziptrace"}
This task makes forensic paths concrete. The path
<span class="forensic-path">1048576-GZIP-0</span> told us: go to byte 1,048,576
in the image, find a GZIP stream, decompress it, and the artifact is at offset 0.

We traced each segment manually. First, `dd` extracts bytes at the image offset.
Piping to `file` confirms it is gzip compressed data. Then `gunzip` decompresses the
stream, revealing the hidden communication log with `operator_x@securemail.onion`
right at the beginning (offset 0).

bulk_extractor's recursive decompression scanners did this automatically during
Phase 1. When the GZIP scanner detected a valid gzip signature, it decompressed
the data into a temporary buffer and re-submitted it to all other scanners. The
email scanner then found the addresses in the decompressed content and recorded
the compound forensic path.

The same mechanism works for ZIP archives, BASE64 encoding, Windows hibernation
files (HIBER), and other container formats. Each decompression layer adds a segment
to the forensic path, creating a complete trail back to the original bytes.
:::

::::

::: {.try-it}
**Try it:** Repeat this process for a `BASE64` path from `email.txt`. Extract the
raw bytes at the base image offset, decode with `base64 -d`, and verify you can
find the email at the indicated sub-offset. How does the decoded content compare
to what you see in the `.b64` file on the filesystem?
:::


<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
<!-- TASK 13 — Capstone Triage Report                            -->
<!-- ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->

```{=html}
<div class="sticky-header"><h2>Task 13: Capstone — Triage Report</h2></div>
```

::: {.capstone-box}
**Scenario:** You have been asked to perform an initial triage of `be_lab.dd` and produce
a brief intelligence summary for the lead investigator. Your report should synthesize
findings from bulk_extractor output, filesystem analysis, and cross-referencing.
:::

Use every technique from this tutorial to compile your report. The following one-liners
will help you extract specific intelligence:

```bash
# Count unique email addresses
cut -f2 be_output/email.txt | grep -v '^#' | sort -u | wc -l

# Extract all .ru / .onion / privacy-focused domains
cut -f2 be_output/domain.txt | grep -v '^#' | \
    grep -E '\.(ru|onion|ch|de)$' | sort -u

# Find features from unallocated space (offsets not in any file)
# After running identify_filenames.py:
grep '(unallocated)' be_output/annotated/email.txt

# List all credit card numbers found
cut -f2 be_output/ccn.txt | grep -v '^#' | sort -u

# Cross-reference phone numbers with sources
paste <(cut -f1 be_output/telephone.txt) \
      <(cut -f2 be_output/telephone.txt) | grep -v '^#'
```

::::{.cr-section}

:::{#cr-capstone}
**Your triage report should address:**

1. **Communication profile:** What email addresses and domains were found?
   Which suggest privacy-conscious behavior?

2. **Financial artifacts:** What credit card numbers, bank routing numbers,
   or wire transfer details were recovered?

3. **Location intelligence:** What GPS coordinates or physical locations
   are referenced? What device took the photo?

4. **Hidden data:** What did recursive decompression and BASE64 decoding
   reveal that a filesystem-only search would miss?

5. **Deleted evidence:** What was in the deleted file? What were the
   suspect's instructions?

6. **Unallocated artifacts:** What was found in the swap-like fragment?
   What does this suggest about other data that may have existed?

7. **Investigative leads:** Based on all findings, what specific follow-up
   actions would you recommend?
:::

:::{focus-on="cr-capstone"}
This exercise integrates everything. You are not just running tools — you are
synthesizing outputs from bulk_extractor (feature files, histograms, carved data),
TSK (filesystem listing, deleted inode recovery), and shell utilities (grep, cut,
sort) into a coherent investigative narrative.

A good triage report is concise: one to two pages that tell the lead investigator
what was found, what it means, and what to do next. Cite specific evidence — byte
offsets, filenames, feature file entries — so every claim is verifiable.

This is the workflow you will use on real forensic images. The image will be larger,
the artifacts more numerous, and the noise louder — but the process is identical:
scan, read histograms, examine features, bridge to filesystem context, and
synthesize.
:::

::::


## Summary

You have now completed a full bulk_extractor workflow:

  - **Task 0:** Established filesystem ground truth with TSK
  - **Tasks 1–2:** Ran a full scan and explored the output structure
  - **Tasks 3–4:** Read feature files and interpreted forensic paths
  - **Task 5:** Used histograms for frequency-based triage
  - **Task 6:** Ran targeted scans with scanner selection flags
  - **Task 7:** Searched for case-specific patterns with `-F`
  - **Task 8:** Analyzed carved JPEG metadata with EXIF data
  - **Task 9:** Built and applied stop lists for noise reduction
  - **Task 10:** Bridged bulk_extractor output to filesystem context
  - **Task 11:** Recovered deleted file content via byte offsets
  - **Task 12:** Manually traced a forensic path through GZIP decompression
  - **Task 13:** Synthesized all findings into an investigative triage report

The central lesson: **bulk_extractor and TSK are complementary.** bulk_extractor
finds artifacts that filesystem tools cannot see — compressed, encoded, deleted,
fragmented. TSK provides the file attribution and directory context that
bulk_extractor cannot. Professional forensic analysis uses both.
